**The Giant's Slumber**

Long ago, before mountains touched the sky, the earth was flat and endless. The first giant, Morvath, wandered this vast plain, lonely and weary. He had shaped the rivers with his fingers and carved valleys with his footsteps, but found no rest.

One day, Morvath grew so tired that he lay down to sleep, promising himself just a brief rest. But his exhaustion ran deeper than oceans. As he slept, his body became heavy, sinking into the soft earth. Days passed, then seasons, then centuries.

Rain fell upon his sleeping form, and soil gathered on his massive body. Grass grew over him, then trees. His breath became the wind that whistled through hidden caves. His heartbeat slowed to match the rhythm of the earth itself.

The other giants searched for Morvath but never found him. They didn't recognize the great peaks that had risen where he lay—his shoulders forming twin summits, his spine a mighty ridge stretching toward the horizon.

Now, when avalanches rumble down mountainsides, the old ones say it is Morvath stirring in his endless dream. And they wonder: will he ever wake, or will the mountains stand eternal, a monument to the giant who finally found his rest?

**The First Mirror**

Long ago, when the world was young, there lived a lonely goddess named Talia who dwelt above the clouds. She had created mountains, rivers, and forests, but had never seen her own face.

One day, Talia wept from loneliness, and her tears fell to earth, forming the first lake. Curious, she descended to see what she had made. When she gazed into the still water, she gasped—for the first time, she saw herself.

But the reflection smiled differently than she did. It spoke: "I am Echo, your twin, trapped in the world of reflections. Every mirror, every pool of water is my prison and my home."

Talia reached toward the water, but Echo pulled back. "We can never touch," Echo warned. "If we do, both worlds will shatter."

From that day forward, Talia created humans to keep Echo company. She gave them faces that would reflect in water, so Echo would never be alone. And that is why, when you look into a mirror or a still pond, you see yourself—but you're really seeing Echo, Talia's forgotten twin, who watches from behind every reflection, waiting patiently through the ages.
